followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: First cycle; no prior review to compare against.

main_claims.claim: Quasicrystals with self-similarity (quasi-periodicity, non-crystallographic orientational symmetry, discrete scale invariance) can be systematically generalized from Euclidean to Lorentzian spacetime via a symmetric cut-and-project scheme based on hyperbolic Coxeter theory.
main_claims.notes: The generalization is technically natural and the three-step construction (Choice 1: Lorentzian lattice; Choice 2: decomposition invariant under Coxeter element subgroup; Choice 3: rotationally-symmetric window or weighting function) is clearly laid out and consistent with the Euclidean framework in Section III.

main_claims.claim: The non-crystallographic orientational symmetry group of a spacetime quasicrystal is infinite (whereas it is finite for Euclidean quasicrystals), because hyperbolic Coxeter elements have infinite order.
main_claims.notes: Correct: for finite/spherical Coxeter groups the Coxeter number h is finite; for hyperbolic Coxeter groups it is infinite. The group generated by the Coxeter element therefore has infinite order in the Lorentzian case. This is a genuine and important distinction.

main_claims.claim: The largest eigenvalue of a bicolorable hyperbolic Coxeter element is a Salem number.
main_claims.notes: Attributed to A'Campo (1976) [Ref. 1]. The claim is standard in the literature on Salem numbers and hyperbolic Coxeter groups. No gaps detected.

main_claims.claim: Spacetime quasicrystals (with infinite orientational symmetry) cannot have local scale invariance (LIDS), cannot be promoted to tilings, and have no preferred window size.
main_claims.notes: No tiling: argument in IV.3.1 is geometrically plausible but informal (infinite orbit argument). No local scale invariance (IV.3.3): uses the established criterion that inflated window must decompose as finite unions/intersections of translated copies—the argument that a spherically-symmetric ball cannot satisfy this is geometrically intuitive but not rigorously proved. No preferred window size (IV.3.2): asserted rather than proved.

main_claims.claim: Spacetime quasicrystals can be self-dual or globally scale-invariant via the weighting scheme, and these are the most natural constructions in the Lorentzian setting.
main_claims.notes: Follows from the Euclidean theory in Section III.1.3 and III.3.6 applied to the Lorentzian case. The preference for the weighting over the window scheme is well-motivated by the lack of a preferred window size.

main_claims.claim: 4D Minkowski spacetime can be embedded as an irrationally-sloped dense slice of a 10D Lorentzian torus (the II_{9,1} compactification), avoiding closed timelike curves for observers confined to the 4D slice.
main_claims.notes: The mathematical claim that the irrational 4D subspace does not intersect the lattice (except at the origin) follows from non-crystallographicity. However, the claim that observers would perceive 'no closed timelike curves' is asserted without proof. The causal structure of the 4D subspace inside the 10D torus (which does contain CTCs globally) requires careful analysis that is absent.

main_claims.claim: A 'volume seesaw' argument with n=6 extra dimensions explains the observed seesaw relation M_{EW}^2 ~ M_{Pl} * M_Lambda between the electroweak, Planck, and vacuum energy scales.
main_claims.notes: The derivation has a dimensional inconsistency: Eq. (59) sets V_{3D} * V_perp = V_T, but V_{3D} ~ H^{-3} has dimensions [L]^3 while V_T = R^n has dimensions [L]^n, making their product [L]^{3+n} != [L]^n. Tracing through the algebra with n=6, the derivation yields M_{Pl}^8 = M_{EW}^8 * H^{-3} (dimensionally inconsistent), not the claimed seesaw M_{EW}^2 ~ M_{Pl} * M_Lambda. Even granting the heuristic intent, the final scaling does not cleanly reduce to the stated seesaw relation.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Uniform filling of the extra-dimensional torus by the 3-brane (ergodicity), needed to justify the volume seesaw V_{3D} * V_perp = V_T.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The equation implicitly assumes that the thickened 3-brane uniformly tiles the total torus volume. This ergodic equidistribution follows from the irrationality of the embedding, but requires a quantitative statement about the rate of equidistribution (related to how quickly the dense winding fills the torus) to give a meaningful finite-time or finite-volume statement. Without this, the 'volume seesaw' is an asymptotic statement that does not fix a unique relationship between scales.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The higher-dimensional Planck mass M_D equals the electroweak scale M_{EW} (no new hierarchy problem introduced).
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The paper imports this from ADD models (Arkani-Hamed, Dimopoulos, Dvali 1998 [Ref. 4]) as an additional assumption. This assumption merely restates the goal (explaining the Planck-EW hierarchy) rather than deriving it. If M_D != M_{EW}, the seesaw argument fails. The paper should more clearly flag this as a circular step.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The de Sitter horizon volume H^{-3} is the correct interpretation of the finite 3-volume of the 3-brane (equating a cosmological observable with an internal geometric quantity).
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The identification of the finite 3-brane volume with the de Sitter horizon volume involves equating two quantities with very different physical origins. The de Sitter horizon is an observer-dependent concept in classical GR, whereas the 3-brane volume is a geometric property of the embedding. A physical mechanism explaining why these two quantities are equal is absent.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Gravity propagates in the full 10D bulk (is not confined to the 3-brane), while matter fields are confined to the 3-brane.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: This is a fundamental assumption borrowed from large extra dimension models, not derived from the spacetime quasicrystal framework. The mechanism by which standard model fields are confined to the irrationally-sloped 4D subspace is not explained. Without a confinement mechanism, the physical picture is incomplete.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The Coxeter element chosen for the non-crystallographic symmetry is the most natural or relevant choice; other choices of non-crystallographic subgroup might yield different physical predictions.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The paper explicitly acknowledges that classifying all non-crystallographic subgroups of the lattice automorphism group is left for future work. The physical conclusions in Section V depend on the specific choice of the Coxeter element subgroup and on the specific II_{9,1} decomposition. Alternative choices could yield different scale hierarchies.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the dimensional analysis in Section V.1.3 (Eq. 57-65) actually consistent when worked through carefully in Planck units or some other natural unit system? Does the n=6 case reproduce M_{EW}^2 ~ M_{Pl} * M_Lambda?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper omits the intermediate algebraic steps from Eq. (62)+(64) to the claimed result (65). The model's own calculation yields a different scaling relation for n=6. An expert in extra-dimensional phenomenology (ADD models) should verify whether there is a consistent reading of the equations that gives the stated seesaw.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: For the bicolorable hyperbolic Coxeter groups relevant to II_{9,1}, does A'Campo's theorem (Ref. [1]) directly imply that the leading eigenvalue of the Coxeter element is a Salem number, or are additional conditions required?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: A'Campo (1976) works in a specific algebraic setting. Verifying that the theorem's hypotheses are satisfied for the Coxeter element of II_{9,1} requires checking the specific polynomial—which the paper tabulates numerically but not symbolically—against A'Campo's conditions.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is there a physically consistent mechanism within the spacetime quasicrystal framework that confines standard model matter to the 4D irrationally-sloped subspace, as required for the hierarchy argument in Section V.1.3?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This is a model-building question in quantum field theory and string theory. The paper does not provide such a mechanism; evaluating whether one exists requires expertise in extra-dimensional model building beyond what can be assessed from the text alone.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Do the Coxeter-Dynkin diagram corrections to Vinberg's original papers (claimed in Appendix A) agree with independent reproductions of Vinberg's algorithm for p=17-19?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper claims Vinberg's original diagrams contain typos and provides corrected versions, but the original papers are in Russian/German and the corrections cannot be verified without access to those sources and independent recomputation.

novelty_assessment: The mathematical core of the paper is genuinely novel. While Lorentzian/hyperbolic Coxeter theory and self-dual Lorentzian lattices are well-established, their systematic application to construct quasicrystal analogues in Minkowski spacetime—with a careful treatment of the cut-and-project scheme, self-duality, global scale invariance, and the role of Salem numbers—appears to be new. The paper correctly distinguishes its construction from prior works on 'time quasicrystals' (Flicker 2018, Vacaru 2018, etc.) which involve different phenomena. The identification of the relevant non-crystallographic group as the infinite group generated by a hyperbolic Coxeter element, and the resulting features (infinite orientational symmetry, no tiling, no local scale invariance, only global scale invariance), constitute a coherent new mathematical framework. The notions of self-dual and globally-scale-invariant quasicrystals via the weighting scheme also appear to be new contributions to the Euclidean case (Sec. III). The physical speculations in Section V reuse the framework of large extra dimensions and toroidal compactifications (Moore 1993, ADD 1998), with the quasicrystal structure providing a new geometric mechanism, but without deriving any quantitative predictions beyond plausibility arguments. The seesaw relation appears in the literature independently of quasicrystals.
score_rationale: The mathematical sections (II–IV) are technically solid: the Lorentzian sC&P construction is logically coherent, the examples are explicitly computed, and the novel features (infinite symmetry group, no tiling, no LIDS, global scale invariance) are plausibly argued. The framework is a genuine and interesting extension of quasicrystal theory. Novelty is real, and the paper will be of interest to mathematical physicists working on aperiodic tilings, causal sets, and discrete holography. The score is held back from the 7–8 range primarily by Section V, where the seesaw argument (the paper's most concrete physical claim) has a dimensional inconsistency in its key equation that the authors do not resolve, and where key physical assumptions (matter confinement, identification of de Sitter volume with 3-brane volume) are borrowed without derivation. The mathematical sections alone would merit 7–7.5; the incompleteness of the physical argument brings the overall score to 6.0. The paper is suitable for publication as a mathematical physics contribution with the Section V speculations clearly flagged, which the authors have done.
summary: Boyle and Mygdalas extend the theory of self-similar quasicrystals from Euclidean space to Minkowski spacetime. The paper develops a Lorentzian variant of the symmetric cut-and-project (sC&P) scheme by exploiting hyperbolic Coxeter theory, Lorentzian lattices (especially odd and even self-dual lattices), and the spectral theory of Coxeter elements. Key mathematical contributions include: (i) identification of the relevant non-crystallographic symmetry group as the infinite group generated by a Coxeter element of a hyperbolic Coxeter group; (ii) the observation that the associated orientational symmetry group is now infinite (rather than finite as in the Euclidean case); (iii) construction of first explicit examples—a 2D spacetime quasicrystal from I_{1,1} and 4D examples from II_{9,1}; and (iv) a discussion of novel features compared to Euclidean quasicrystals (no associated tiling, no preferred window size, no local scale invariance, but possible global scale invariance and self-duality). The paper ends with speculative physical applications, most notably a scenario where infinite 4D Minkowski spacetime is embedded as an irrationally-sloped slice in a 10D toroidal compactification, and a heuristic seesaw argument attempting to explain hierarchies between the Planck, electroweak, and vacuum energy scales. Section V is explicitly labeled speculative throughout.

technical_objections.evidence: Eq. (57): V_perp = l_Pl^n [units: L^n]. Eq. (58): V_T = R^n [units: L^n]. Eq. (59): V_{3D} * V_perp = V_T implies [L^3][L^n] = [L^n], i.e., [L^3]=1. Tracing through the subsequent algebra yields M_{Pl}^{n+2} = M_{EW}^{n+2} H^{-3} which is dimensionally invalid (H has units [M] so H^{-3} has units [M]^{-3}, and the LHS has units [M]^{n+2}).
technical_objections.objection: The volume seesaw equation (59), V_{3D} * V_perp = V_T, is dimensionally inconsistent as stated. V_{3D} ~ H^{-3} has units [length]^3, V_perp = l_{Pl}^n has units [length]^n, so their product has units [length]^{3+n}, while V_T = R^n has units [length]^n. The equation as written requires [length]^3 = 1, which is only satisfied in Planck units with a specific normalization not explicitly adopted.

technical_objections.evidence: Assuming Planck units to repair the dimensional issue: from (59) H^{-3} = (R/l_{Pl})^6, and ADD gives M_{Pl}^2 = M_{EW}^8 R^6, one obtains M_{Pl}^8 = M_{EW}^8 H^{-3} M_{Pl}^{-6}... which does not reduce to M_{EW}^2 ~ M_{Pl} M_{Lambda} in any transparent way. The authors claim this reduces to the seesaw 'precisely when n=6' but do not show the algebraic steps explicitly.
technical_objections.objection: The claim that n=6 extra dimensions reproduces the seesaw relation M_{EW}^2 ~ M_{Pl} * M_Lambda is not verified by the paper's own equations. Combining the volume seesaw (59) with the ADD relation (64) and Friedmann equation (60) gives a different scaling relation, not the stated seesaw.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper states 'an observer confined to this [4D subspace] would regard themselves as living in infinite (non-compact) 4D Minkowski spacetime, with no closed timelike curves' (text after eq. 51 in Sec. V.1.1). No proof or detailed argument is given. The Minkowski metric restricted to the 4D subspace might be free of CTCs, but the paper does not analyze this.
technical_objections.objection: The absence of closed timelike curves for observers on the 4D slice is asserted but not proved. The 10D torus with the Lorentzian metric does contain CTCs (since time is compactified). Whether an observer confined to the 4D irrationally-sloped slice encounters CTCs depends on whether any timelike geodesic of the 4D slice, when lifted to the 10D torus, closes up.

technical_objections.evidence: Appendix A states: 'in dimensions p=17,18,19... the Coxeter graphs... are no longer bicolorable (except when p=1) since they contain at least one closed loop of odd length.' The main construction in Sec. IV uses bicolorability to define separate red/blue generators and the Coxeter element as their product.
technical_objections.objection: The spectral and sC&P results in Section IV.1.3 and IV.2 are developed under the assumption that the Coxeter diagram is bicolorable. But the Appendix A notes that for dimensions p=17, 18, 19, the Coxeter diagrams of the odd self-dual lattices I_{p,1} contain odd-length loops and are not bicolorable (except when p=1). The paper does not address what modifications the non-bicolorable case requires.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section IV.1.3, Appendix A (description of p=17-19 cases)

technical_objections.evidence: The paper states the Coxeter element generates a 'non-crystallographic subgroup' and that 'total irrationality of E_ph and E_in now follows from the fact that G is non-crystallographic.' But the argument that the infinite cyclic group generated by a hyperbolic Coxeter element is non-crystallographic (i.e., cannot stabilize a lattice) is not given.
technical_objections.objection: The non-crystallographicity of the group generated by the Coxeter element (necessary for the sC&P construction to yield a quasicrystal rather than a periodic structure) is asserted but not proved for the Lorentzian case.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper states 'a spherically-symmetric window cannot be expressed as a finite number of unions and intersections of translated copies of itself (after rescaling).' This is geometrically obvious for compact balls but less obvious for the potentially non-compact setting discussed.
technical_objections.objection: The claim that spacetime quasicrystals can have no local scale invariance relies on the fact that a spherically-symmetric ball cannot be expressed as a finite number of unions and intersections of translated copies of itself (after rescaling). The paper cites the condition from [7,5] but does not prove it applies here; specifically, the condition in [7,5] is for polyhedral windows, and it is unclear whether it extends to infinite-radius balls in internal spaces with Euclidean metric.
